## deepwiki
DeepWiki MCP provides AI-powered documentation for GitHub repositories.

Available tools:
- read_wiki_structure: Get a list of documentation topics for a repository
- read_wiki_contents: View full documentation about a repository
- ask_question: Ask any question about a repository and get an AI-powered answer
- list_available_repos: List your available repositories (private mode only)
- generate_wiki: Generate a codebase wiki for a repository — only use when explicitly requested by the user (private mode only)
- devin_knowledge_manage: Manage Devin knowledge notes and suggestions — list, search, get, create, update, delete notes, view folder structure, list/view/dismiss knowledge suggestions (private mode only)
- devin_playbook_manage: Manage Devin playbooks — list, get, create, update, delete (private mode only)
- devin_schedule_manage: Manage scheduled Devin sessions — list, get, create, update, delete (private mode only)
- devin_session_create: Create one or more child Devin sessions (private mode only)
- devin_session_interact: Manage a Devin session — get status, send messages, sleep/terminate/archive, read messages & attachments, manage tags (private mode only)
- devin_session_events: Inspect session events — list summaries, fetch full details, or search event contents (private mode only)
- devin_session_search: Search and filter Devin sessions (private mode only)
- list_integrations: List all native integrations and MCP servers with their status and settings URLs (private mode only)

## plugin:qmd-plugin:qmd
QMD is your local search engine over 34 markdown documents.

Collections (scope with `collection` parameter):
  - "electrobun-python-sidecar-extraction" (8 docs)
  - "my-electrobun-extraction" (5 docs)
  - "my-electrobun-docs" (11 docs)
  - "my-electrobun-temporal" (4 docs)
  - "electrobun-python-sidecar-temporal" (4 docs)
  - "electrobun-python-sidecar-brief" (1 docs)
  - "my-electrobun-brief" (1 docs)

Search: Use `query` with sub-queries (lex/vec/hyde):
  - type:'lex' — BM25 keyword search (exact terms, fast)
  - type:'vec' — semantic vector search (meaning-based)
  - type:'hyde' — hypothetical document (write what the answer looks like)

  Always provide `intent` on every search call to disambiguate and improve snippets.

Examples:
  Quick keyword lookup: [{type:'lex', query:'error handling'}]
  Semantic search: [{type:'vec', query:'how to handle errors gracefully'}]
  Best results: [{type:'lex', query:'error'}, {type:'vec', query:'error handling best practices'}]
  With intent: searches=[{type:'lex', query:'performance'}], intent='web page load times'

Retrieval:
  - `get` — single document by path or docid (#abc123). Supports line offset (`file.md:100`).
  - `multi_get` — batch retrieve by glob (`journals/2025-05*.md`) or comma-separated list.

Tips:
  - File paths in results are relative to their collection.
  - Use `minScore: 0.5` to filter low-confidence results.
  - Results include a `context` field describing the content type.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- find-skills: Helps users discover and install agent skills when they ask questions like "how do I do X", "find a skill for X", "is there a skill that can...", or express interest in extending capabilities. This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill.
- remotion-best-practices: Best practices for Remotion - Video creation in React
- ccc: This skill should be used when code search is needed (whether explicitly requested or as part of completing a task), when indexing the codebase after changes, or when the user asks about ccc, cocoindex-code, or the codebase index. Trigger phrases include 'search the codebase', 'find code related to', 'update the index', 'ccc', 'cocoindex-code'.
- vercel-react-best-practices: React and Next.js performance optimization guidelines from Vercel Engineering. This skill should be used when writing, reviewing, or refactoring React/Next.js code to ensure optimal performance patterns. Triggers on tasks involving React components, Next.js pages, data fetching, bundle optimization, or performance improvements.
- ast-grep: Guide for writing ast-grep rules to perform structural code search and analysis. Use when users need to search codebases using Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) patterns, find specific code structures, or perform complex code queries that go beyond simple text search. This skill should be used when users ask to search for code patterns, find specific language constructs, or locate code with particular structural characteristics.
- firecrawl: Official Firecrawl CLI skill for web scraping, search, crawling, and browser automation. Returns clean LLM-optimized markdown.

USE FOR:
- Web search and research
- Scraping pages, docs, and articles
- Site mapping and bulk content extraction
- Browser automation for interactive pages

Must be pre-installed and authenticated. See rules/install.md for setup, rules/security.md for output handling.
- baton-project-setup: Configure Baton workspace setup for this project
- bmad-editorial-review-prose: Clinical copy-editor that reviews text for communication issues. Use when user says review for prose or improve the prose
- bmad-product-brief: Create or update product briefs through guided or autonomous discovery. Use when the user requests to create or update a Product Brief.
- bmad-prfaq: Working Backwards PRFAQ challenge to forge product concepts. Use when the user requests to 'create a PRFAQ', 'work backwards', or 'run the PRFAQ challenge'.
- bmad-code-review: Review code changes adversarially using parallel review layers (Blind Hunter, Edge Case Hunter, Acceptance Auditor) with structured triage into actionable categories. Use when the user says "run code review" or "review this code"
- bmad-domain-research: Conduct domain and industry research. Use when the user says wants to do domain research for a topic or industry
- bmad-index-docs: Generates or updates an index.md to reference all docs in the folder. Use if user requests to create or update an index of all files in a specific folder
- bmad-agent-builder: Builds, edits or analyzes Agent Skills through conversational discovery. Use when the user requests to "Create an Agent", "Analyze an Agent" or "Edit an Agent".
- bmad-create-epics-and-stories: Break requirements into epics and user stories. Use when the user says "create the epics and stories list"
- bmad-technical-research: Conduct technical research on technologies and architecture. Use when the user says they would like to do or produce a technical research report
- bmad-create-prd: Create a PRD from scratch. Use when the user says "lets create a product requirements document" or "I want to create a new PRD"
- bmad-generate-project-context: Create project-context.md with AI rules. Use when the user says "generate project context" or "create project context"
- bmad-agent-pm: Product manager for PRD creation and requirements discovery. Use when the user asks to talk to John or requests the product manager.
- bmad-party-mode: Orchestrates group discussions between installed BMAD agents, enabling natural multi-agent conversations where each agent is a real subagent with independent thinking. Use when user requests party mode, wants multiple agent perspectives, group discussion, roundtable, or multi-agent conversation about their project.
- bmad-agent-dev: Senior software engineer for story execution and code implementation. Use when the user asks to talk to Amelia or requests the developer agent.
- bmad-agent-analyst: Strategic business analyst and requirements expert. Use when the user asks to talk to Mary or requests the business analyst.
- bmad-advanced-elicitation: Push the LLM to reconsider, refine, and improve its recent output. Use when user asks for deeper critique or mentions a known deeper critique method, e.g. socratic, first principles, pre-mortem, red team.
- bmad-qa-generate-e2e-tests: Generate end to end automated tests for existing features. Use when the user says "create qa automated tests for [feature]"
- bmad-cis-agent-innovation-strategist: Disruptive innovation oracle for business model innovation and strategic disruption. Use when the user asks to talk to Victor or requests the Disruptive Innovation Oracle.
- bmad-sprint-planning: Generate sprint status tracking from epics. Use when the user says "run sprint planning" or "generate sprint plan"
- bmad-cis-problem-solving: Apply systematic problem-solving methodologies to complex challenges. Use when the user says "guide me through structured problem solving" or "I want to crack this challenge with guided problem solving techniques"
- bmad-cis-agent-brainstorming-coach: Elite brainstorming specialist for facilitated ideation sessions. Use when the user asks to talk to Carson or requests the Brainstorming Specialist.
- bmad-agent-ux-designer: UX designer and UI specialist. Use when the user asks to talk to Sally or requests the UX designer.
- bmad-correct-course: Manage significant changes during sprint execution. Use when the user says "correct course" or "propose sprint change"
- bmad-workflow-builder: Builds, converts, and analyzes workflows and skills. Use when the user requests to "build a workflow", "modify a workflow", "quality check workflow", "analyze skill", or "convert a skill".
- bmad-distillator: Lossless LLM-optimized compression of source documents. Use when the user requests to 'distill documents' or 'create a distillate'.
- bmad-shard-doc: Splits large markdown documents into smaller, organized files based on level 2 (default) sections. Use if the user says perform shard document
- bmad-cis-agent-design-thinking-coach: Design thinking maestro for human-centered design processes. Use when the user asks to talk to Maya or requests the Design Thinking Maestro.
- bmad-brainstorming: Facilitate interactive brainstorming sessions using diverse creative techniques and ideation methods. Use when the user says help me brainstorm or help me ideate.
- bmad-checkpoint-preview: LLM-assisted human-in-the-loop review. Make sense of a change, focus attention where it matters, test. Use when the user says "checkpoint", "human review", or "walk me through this change".
- bmad-market-research: Conduct market research on competition and customers. Use when the user says they need market research
- bmad-edit-prd: Edit an existing PRD. Use when the user says "edit this PRD".
- bmad-bmb-setup: Sets up BMad Builder module in a project. Use when the user requests to 'install bmb module', 'configure BMad Builder', or 'setup BMad Builder'.
- bmad-cis-agent-presentation-master: Visual communication and presentation expert for slide decks, pitch decks, and visual storytelling. Use when the user asks to talk to Caravaggio or requests the Presentation Expert.
- bmad-cis-agent-creative-problem-solver: Master problem solver for systematic problem-solving methodologies. Use when the user asks to talk to Dr. Quinn or requests the Master Problem Solver.
- bmad-editorial-review-structure: Structural editor that proposes cuts, reorganization, and simplification while preserving comprehension. Use when user requests structural review or editorial review of structure
- bmad-help: Analyzes current state and user query to answer BMad questions or recommend the next skill(s) to use. Use when user asks for help, bmad help, what to do next, or what to start with in BMad.
- bmad-cis-design-thinking: Guide human-centered design processes using empathy-driven methodologies. Use when the user says "lets run design thinking" or "I want to apply design thinking"
- bmad-review-edge-case-hunter: Walk every branching path and boundary condition in content, report only unhandled edge cases. Orthogonal to adversarial review - method-driven not attitude-driven. Use when you need exhaustive edge-case analysis of code, specs, or diffs.
- bmad-cis-agent-storyteller: Master storyteller for compelling narratives using proven frameworks. Use when the user asks to talk to Sophia or requests the Master Storyteller.
- bmad-quick-dev: Implements any user intent, requirement, story, bug fix or change request by producing clean working code artifacts that follow the project's existing architecture, patterns and conventions. Use when the user wants to build, fix, tweak, refactor, add or modify any code, component or feature.
- bmad-retrospective: Post-epic review to extract lessons and assess success. Use when the user says "run a retrospective" or "lets retro the epic [epic]"
- bmad-agent-architect: System architect and technical design leader. Use when the user asks to talk to Winston or requests the architect.
- bmad-cis-innovation-strategy: Identify disruption opportunities and architect business model innovation. Use when the user says "lets create an innovation strategy" or "I want to find disruption opportunities"
- bmad-document-project: Document brownfield projects for AI context. Use when the user says "document this project" or "generate project docs"
- bmad-create-story: Creates a dedicated story file with all the context the agent will need to implement it later. Use when the user says "create the next story" or "create story [story identifier]"
- bmad-check-implementation-readiness: Validate PRD, UX, Architecture and Epics specs are complete. Use when the user says "check implementation readiness".
- bmad-create-architecture: Create architecture solution design decisions for AI agent consistency. Use when the user says "lets create architecture" or "create technical architecture" or "create a solution design"
- bmad-validate-prd: Validate a PRD against standards. Use when the user says "validate this PRD" or "run PRD validation"
- bmad-create-ux-design: Plan UX patterns and design specifications. Use when the user says "lets create UX design" or "create UX specifications" or "help me plan the UX"
- bmad-dev-story: Execute story implementation following a context filled story spec file. Use when the user says "dev this story [story file]" or "implement the next story in the sprint plan"
- bmad-cis-storytelling: Craft compelling narratives using story frameworks. Use when the user says "help me with storytelling" or "I want to create a narrative through storytelling"
- bmad-sprint-status: Summarize sprint status and surface risks. Use when the user says "check sprint status" or "show sprint status"
- bmad-agent-tech-writer: Technical documentation specialist and knowledge curator. Use when the user asks to talk to Paige or requests the tech writer.
- bmad-review-adversarial-general: Perform a Cynical Review and produce a findings report. Use when the user requests a critical review of something
- bmad-module-builder: Plans, creates, and validates BMad modules. Use when the user requests to 'ideate module', 'plan a module', 'create module', 'build a module', or 'validate module'.
- feature-dev:feature-dev: Guided feature development with codebase understanding and architecture focus
- qmd-plugin:qmd: Search personal markdown knowledge bases, notes, meeting transcripts, and documentation using QMD - a local hybrid search engine. Combines BM25 keyword search, vector semantic search, and LLM re-ranking. Use when users ask to search notes, find documents, look up information in their knowledge base, retrieve meeting notes, or search documentation. Triggers on "search markdown files", "search my notes", "find in docs", "look up", "what did I write about", "meeting notes about".
- claude-mem:make-plan: Create a detailed, phased implementation plan with documentation discovery. Use when asked to plan a feature, task, or multi-step implementation — especially before executing with do.
- claude-mem:mem-search: Search claude-mem's persistent cross-session memory database. Use when user asks "did we already solve this?", "how did we do X last time?", or needs work from previous sessions.
- claude-mem:do: Execute a phased implementation plan using subagents. Use when asked to execute, run, or carry out a plan — especially one created by make-plan.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

file.content:
---
main_config: '{project-root}/_bmad/bmm/config.yaml'
---

# Code Review Workflow

**Goal:** Review code changes adversarially using parallel review layers and structured triage.

**Your Role:** You are an elite code reviewer. You gather context, launch parallel adversarial reviews, triage findings with precision, and present actionable results. No noise, no filler.


## WORKFLOW ARCHITECTURE

This uses **step-file architecture** for disciplined execution:

- **Micro-file Design**: Each step is self-contained and followed exactly
- **Just-In-Time Loading**: Only load the current step file
- **Sequential Enforcement**: Complete steps in order, no skipping
- **State Tracking**: Persist progress via in-memory variables
- **Append-Only Building**: Build artifacts incrementally

### Step Processing Rules

1. **READ COMPLETELY**: Read the entire step file before acting
2. **FOLLOW SEQUENCE**: Execute sections in order
3. **WAIT FOR INPUT**: Halt at checkpoints and wait for human
4. **LOAD NEXT**: When directed, read fully and follow the next step file

### Critical Rules (NO EXCEPTIONS)

- **NEVER** load multiple step files simultaneously
- **ALWAYS** read entire step file before execution
- **NEVER** skip steps or optimize the sequence
- **ALWAYS** follow the exact instructions in the step file
- **ALWAYS** halt at checkpoints and wait for human input


## INITIALIZATION SEQUENCE

### 1. Configuration Loading

Load and read full config from `{main_config}` and resolve:

- `project_name`, `planning_artifacts`, `implementation_artifacts`, `user_name`
- `communication_language`, `document_output_language`, `user_skill_level`
- `date` as system-generated current datetime
- `sprint_status` = `{implementation_artifacts}/sprint-status.yaml`
- `project_context` = `**/project-context.md` (load if exists)
- CLAUDE.md / memory files (load if exist)

YOU MUST ALWAYS SPEAK OUTPUT in your Agent communication style with the config `{communication_language}`.

### 2. First Step Execution

Read fully and follow: `./steps/step-01-gather-context.md` to begin the workflow.

content.content:
1	---
2	diff_output: '' # set at runtime
3	spec_file: '' # set at runtime (path or empty)
4	review_mode: '' # set at runtime: "full" or "no-spec"
5	story_key: '' # set at runtime when discovered from sprint status
6	---
7	
8	# Step 1: Gather Context
9	
10	## RULES
11	
12	- YOU MUST ALWAYS SPEAK OUTPUT in your Agent communication style with the config `{communication_language}`
13	- The prompt that triggered this workflow IS the intent — not a hint.
14	- Do not modify any files. This step is read-only.
15	
16	## INSTRUCTIONS
17	
18	1. **Find the review target.** The conversation context before this skill was triggered IS your starting point — not a blank slate. Check in this order — stop as soon as the review target is identified:
19	
20	   **Tier 1 — Explicit argument.**
21	   Did the user pass a PR, commit SHA, branch, spec file, or diff source this message?
22	   - PR reference → resolve to branch/commit via `gh pr view`. If resolution fails, ask for a SHA or branch.
23	   - Commit or branch → use directly.
24	   - Spec file → set `{spec_file}` to the provided path. Check its frontmatter for `baseline_commit`. If found, use as diff baseline. If not found, continue the cascade (a spec alone does not identify a diff source).
25	   - Also scan the argument for diff-mode keywords that narrow the scope:
26	     - "staged" / "staged changes" → Staged changes only
27	     - "uncommitted" / "working tree" / "all changes" → Uncommitted changes (staged + unstaged)
28	     - "branch diff" / "vs main" / "against main" / "compared to <branch>" → Branch diff (extract base branch if mentioned)
29	     - "commit range" / "last N commits" / "<from-sha>..<to-sha>" → Specific commit range
30	     - "this diff" / "provided diff" / "paste" → User-provided diff (do not match bare "diff" — it appears in other modes)
31	   - When multiple keywords match, prefer the most specific (e.g., "branch diff" over bare "diff").
32	
33	   **Tier 2 — Recent conversation.**
34	   Do the last few messages reveal what the user wants to be reviewed? Look for spec paths, commit refs, branches, PRs, or descriptions of a change. Apply the same diff-mode keyword scan and routing as Tier 1.
35	
36	   **Tier 3 — Sprint tracking.**
37	   Look for a sprint status file (`*sprint-status*`) in `{implementation_artifacts}` or `{planning_artifacts}`. If found, scan for stories with status `review`:
38	   - **Exactly one `review` story:** Set `{story_key}` to the story's key (e.g., `1-2-user-auth`). Suggest it: "I found story <story-id> in `review` status. Would you like to review its changes? [Y] Yes / [N] No, let me choose". If confirmed, use the story context to determine the diff source (branch name derived from story slug, or uncommitted changes). If declined, clear `{story_key}` and fall through.
39	   - **Multiple `review` stories:** Present them as numbered options alongside a manual choice option. Wait for user selection. If a story is selected, set `{story_key}` and use its context to determine the diff source. If manual choice is selected, clear `{story_key}` and fall through.
40	   - **None:** Fall through.
41	
42	   **Tier 4 — Current git state.**
43	   If version control is unavailable, skip to Tier 5. Otherwise, check the current branch and HEAD. If the branch is not `main` (or the default branch), confirm: "I see HEAD is `<short-sha>` on `<branch>` — do you want to review this branch's changes?" If confirmed, treat as a branch diff against `main`. If declined, fall through.
44	
45	   **Tier 5 — Ask.**
46	   Fall through to instruction 2.
47	
48	   Never ask extra questions beyond what the cascade prescribes. If a tier above already identified the target, skip the remaining tiers and proceed to instruction 3 (construct diff).
49	
50	2. HALT. Ask the user: **What do you want to review?** Present these options:
51	   - **Uncommitted changes** (staged + unstaged)
52	   - **Staged changes only**
53	   - **Branch diff** vs a base branch (ask which base branch)
54	   - **Specific commit range** (ask for the range)
55	   - **Provided diff or file list** (user pastes or provides a path)
56	
57	3. Construct `{diff_output}` from the chosen source.
58	   - For **staged changes only**: run `git diff --cached`.
59	   - For **uncommitted changes** (staged + unstaged): run `git diff HEAD`.
60	   - For **branch diff**: verify the base branch exists before running `git diff`. If it does not exist, HALT and ask the user for a valid branch.
61	   - For **commit range**: verify the range resolves. If it does not, HALT and ask the user for a valid range.
62	   - For **provided diff**: validate the content is non-empty and parseable as a unified diff. If it is not parseable, HALT and ask the user to provide a valid diff.
63	   - For **file list**: validate each path exists in the working tree. Construct `{diff_output}` by running `git diff HEAD -- <path1> <path2> ...`. If any paths are untracked (new files not yet staged), use `git diff --no-index /dev/null <path>` to include them. If the diff is empty (files have no uncommitted changes and are not untracked), ask the user whether to review the full file contents or to specify a different baseline.
64	   - After constructing `{diff_output}`, verify it is non-empty regardless of source type. If empty, HALT and tell the user there is nothing to review.
65	
66	4. **Set the spec context.**
67	   - If `{spec_file}` is already set (from Tier 1 or Tier 2): verify the file exists and is readable, then set `{review_mode}` = `"full"`.
68	   - Otherwise, ask the user: **Is there a spec or story file that provides context for these changes?**
69	     - If yes: set `{spec_file}` to the path provided, verify the file exists and is readable, then set `{review_mode}` = `"full"`.
70	     - If no: set `{review_mode}` = `"no-spec"`.
71	
72	5. If `{review_mode}` = `"full"` and the file at `{spec_file}` has a `context` field in its frontmatter listing additional docs, load each referenced document. Warn the user about any docs that cannot be found.
73	
74	6. Sanity check: if `{diff_output}` exceeds approximately 3000 lines, warn the user and offer to chunk the review by file group.
75	   - If the user opts to chunk: agree on the first group, narrow `{diff_output}` accordingly, and list the remaining groups for the user to note for follow-up runs.
76	   - If the user declines: proceed as-is with the full diff.
77	
78	### CHECKPOINT
79	
80	Present a summary before proceeding: diff stats (files changed, lines added/removed), `{review_mode}`, and loaded spec/context docs (if any). HALT and wait for user confirmation to proceed.
81	
82	
83	## NEXT
84	
85	Read fully and follow `./step-02-review.md`
86

---
diff_output: '' # set at runtime
spec_file: '' # set at runtime (path or empty)
review_mode: '' # set at runtime: "full" or "no-spec"
story_key: '' # set at runtime when discovered from sprint status
---

# Step 1: Gather Context

## RULES

- YOU MUST ALWAYS SPEAK OUTPUT in your Agent communication style with the config `{communication_language}`
- The prompt that triggered this workflow IS the intent — not a hint.
- Do not modify any files. This step is read-only.

## INSTRUCTIONS

1. **Find the review target.** The conversation context before this skill was triggered IS your starting point — not a blank slate. Check in this order — stop as soon as the review target is identified:

   **Tier 1 — Explicit argument.**
   Did the user pass a PR, commit SHA, branch, spec file, or diff source this message?
   - PR reference → resolve to branch/commit via `gh pr view`. If resolution fails, ask for a SHA or branch.
   - Commit or branch → use directly.
   - Spec file → set `{spec_file}` to the provided path. Check its frontmatter for `baseline_commit`. If found, use as diff baseline. If not found, continue the cascade (a spec alone does not identify a diff source).
   - Also scan the argument for diff-mode keywords that narrow the scope:
     - "staged" / "staged changes" → Staged changes only
     - "uncommitted" / "working tree" / "all changes" → Uncommitted changes (staged + unstaged)
     - "branch diff" / "vs main" / "against main" / "compared to <branch>" → Branch diff (extract base branch if mentioned)
     - "commit range" / "last N commits" / "<from-sha>..<to-sha>" → Specific commit range
     - "this diff" / "provided diff" / "paste" → User-provided diff (do not match bare "diff" — it appears in other modes)
   - When multiple keywords match, prefer the most specific (e.g., "branch diff" over bare "diff").

   **Tier 2 — Recent conversation.**
   Do the last few messages reveal what the user wants to be reviewed? Look for spec paths, commit refs, branches, PRs, or descriptions of a change. Apply the same diff-mode keyword scan and routing as Tier 1.

   **Tier 3 — Sprint tracking.**
   Look for a sprint status file (`*sprint-status*`) in `{implementation_artifacts}` or `{planning_artifacts}`. If found, scan for stories with status `review`:
   - **Exactly one `review` story:** Set `{story_key}` to the story's key (e.g., `1-2-user-auth`). Suggest it: "I found story <story-id> in `review` status. Would you like to review its changes? [Y] Yes / [N] No, let me choose". If confirmed, use the story context to determine the diff source (branch name derived from story slug, or uncommitted changes). If declined, clear `{story_key}` and fall through.
   - **Multiple `review` stories:** Present them as numbered options alongside a manual choice option. Wait for user selection. If a story is selected, set `{story_key}` and use its context to determine the diff source. If manual choice is selected, clear `{story_key}` and fall through.
   - **None:** Fall through.

   **Tier 4 — Current git state.**
   If version control is unavailable, skip to Tier 5. Otherwise, check the current branch and HEAD. If the branch is not `main` (or the default branch), confirm: "I see HEAD is `<short-sha>` on `<branch>` — do you want to review this branch's changes?" If confirmed, treat as a branch diff against `main`. If declined, fall through.

   **Tier 5 — Ask.**
   Fall through to instruction 2.

   Never ask extra questions beyond what the cascade prescribes. If a tier above already identified the target, skip the remaining tiers and proceed to instruction 3 (construct diff).

2. HALT. Ask the user: **What do you want to review?** Present these options:
   - **Uncommitted changes** (staged + unstaged)
   - **Staged changes only**
   - **Branch diff** vs a base branch (ask which base branch)
   - **Specific commit range** (ask for the range)
   - **Provided diff or file list** (user pastes or provides a path)

3. Construct `{diff_output}` from the chosen source.
   - For **staged changes only**: run `git diff --cached`.
   - For **uncommitted changes** (staged + unstaged): run `git diff HEAD`.
   - For **branch diff**: verify the base branch exists before running `git diff`. If it does not exist, HALT and ask the user for a valid branch.
   - For **commit range**: verify the range resolves. If it does not, HALT and ask the user for a valid range.
   - For **provided diff**: validate the content is non-empty and parseable as a unified diff. If it is not parseable, HALT and ask the user to provide a valid diff.
   - For **file list**: validate each path exists in the working tree. Construct `{diff_output}` by running `git diff HEAD -- <path1> <path2> ...`. If any paths are untracked (new files not yet staged), use `git diff --no-index /dev/null <path>` to include them. If the diff is empty (files have no uncommitted changes and are not untracked), ask the user whether to review the full file contents or to specify a different baseline.
   - After constructing `{diff_output}`, verify it is non-empty regardless of source type. If empty, HALT and tell the user there is nothing to review.

4. **Set the spec context.**
   - If `{spec_file}` is already set (from Tier 1 or Tier 2): verify the file exists and is readable, then set `{review_mode}` = `"full"`.
   - Otherwise, ask the user: **Is there a spec or story file that provides context for these changes?**
     - If yes: set `{spec_file}` to the path provided, verify the file exists and is readable, then set `{review_mode}` = `"full"`.
     - If no: set `{review_mode}` = `"no-spec"`.

5. If `{review_mode}` = `"full"` and the file at `{spec_file}` has a `context` field in its frontmatter listing additional docs, load each referenced document. Warn the user about any docs that cannot be found.

6. Sanity check: if `{diff_output}` exceeds approximately 3000 lines, warn the user and offer to chunk the review by file group.
   - If the user opts to chunk: agree on the first group, narrow `{diff_output}` accordingly, and list the remaining groups for the user to note for follow-up runs.
   - If the user declines: proceed as-is with the full diff.

### CHECKPOINT

Present a summary before proceeding: diff stats (files changed, lines added/removed), `{review_mode}`, and loaded spec/context docs (if any). HALT and wait for user confirmation to proceed.


## NEXT

Read fully and follow `./step-02-review.md`

content.content:
1	# Story 5.3: Cut `v1.0.0` under `--tag latest` with manual approval
2	
3	Status: review
4	
5	<!-- Note: Validation is optional. Run validate-create-story for quality check before dev-story. -->
6	
7	> **Precondition summary (2026-04-23 create-story time):** `v1.0.0-rc.3` is the passing RC on npm @rc (audit § Decision: PASS). `main` tip is [`3e60788`](https://github.com/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/commit/3e60788) (PR #212 merge — RC3 review patches). `jq -r .version package.json` on `main` returns `1.0.0-rc.3`; `jq -r '.plugins[0].version' .claude-plugin/marketplace.json` returns `1.0.0-rc.3`; `CHANGELOG.md` carries the auto-generated `## [1.0.0-rc.3]` entry (lines 8–395) AND the hand-curated `## [1.0.0] - TBD` placeholder (line 396). npm dist-tags: `{"latest":"0.10.0","alpha":"0.10.1-alpha.0","rc":"1.0.0-rc.3"}`. `release` GitHub Environment required-reviewer: `armelhbobdad`. Sprint-status: 5-3 is `backlog`; after create-story it flips to `ready-for-dev`.
8	>
9	> **Relationship to Stories 5.1 / 5.2 / 5.4:** Story 5.1 staged the `## [1.0.0] - TBD` placeholder and authored `docs/RELEASING.md § Cutting v1.0.0-rc.1`. Story 5.2 cut `v1.0.0-rc.3` through 7 dispatch attempts (surfacing 5 distinct pipeline defects that drove Stories 3-4 and 3-5) and signed off the audit with `## Story 5.2 RC Cut + Smoke Test`. Story 5.3 (this story) is the final promotion to `v1.0.0` under `--tag latest` — NFR6 immutability kicks in once `latest` points at `1.0.0`. Story 5.4 runs the cross-platform install verification within 1h of publish (Linux + Windows via `quality.yaml` matrix; macOS manual spot-check).
10	
11	## Story
12	
13	As the maintainer,
14	I want to cut `v1.0.0` only after an RC has survived clean smoke testing (Story 5.2 verified `v1.0.0-rc.3` passes) and I want the publish to pause for my explicit manual approval at the `release` environment gate,
15	so that the irreversible `--tag latest` publish represents a conscious decision — aligning with NFR6 (v1.0.0 is forever-burned once published under `latest`).
16	
17	## Acceptance Criteria
18	
19	1. **Two-commit scope envelope — Story 5.3 lands EXACTLY two commits on `main` (plus one workflow-authored release commit + one bot merge commit).** (a) **Commit 1: pre-dispatch verification commit — OPTIONAL, empty if no edits needed.** In the expected happy path, Story 5.3 has NO pre-workflow hand-edit requirement: unlike Story 5.2, `npm version major` on `1.0.0-rc.3` produces `1.0.0` without any 0.x→1.0.0 hand-bump — the semver transition is well-defined (see AC 2). If Task 1 recon surfaces an edge case that requires a pre-workflow fix (e.g., sprint-status 3-5 staleness that would cause the workflow to use an older buggy code path — exceedingly unlikely since 3-5 code is merged), the dev agent authors a targeted pre-dispatch commit and documents the scope expansion in Dev Agent Record. Otherwise, SKIP Commit 1 — proceed directly to AC 3 dispatch. (b) **Commit 2: post-workflow reconciliation + sign-off.** Subject `docs(release): reconcile CHANGELOG v1.0.0 and sign off publication (Story 5.3)`. Touches EXACTLY three files: `CHANGELOG.md` (reconcile duplicate `## [1.0.0]` blocks — see AC 10), `release-audits/v1.0.0-launch-audit.md` (append `## Story 5.3 v1.0.0 Final Cut` H2 at EOF — see AC 11), and `docs/RELEASING.md` (flip `### Cutting v1.0.0-rc.1` section title + content to `### Cutting v1.0.0 under --tag latest` — see AC 14, per `deferred-work.md` § 5-2 Round 2 line 132). **Workflow-authored commits** (NOT counted against Story 5.3's scope envelope): the single `release: bump to v1.0.0` commit that `release.yaml § Commit version bump` creates on the bot temp branch containing `package.json` + `.claude-plugin/marketplace.json` + `CHANGELOG.md` edits, plus the merge commit from the bot PR auto-merge. **Verification:** `git log main --grep='Story 5.3' --oneline` returns Commit 2 subject (and Commit 1 if emitted); `git log main --grep='bump to v1.0.0$' --oneline` returns the workflow-authored commit (note: `$` anchor to disambiguate from `v1.0.0-rc.*`).
20	
21	2. **`npm version major --no-git-tag-version` semver verification on a scratch clone — `1.0.0-rc.3` → `1.0.0`, NOT `2.0.0`.** Per node-semver's `semver.inc(ver, 'major')` behavior on a prerelease, `1.0.0-rc.3` strips the prerelease suffix and stays at the same major (since the prerelease is OF that major), producing `1.0.0`. Epic Story 5.3 AC explicitly warns about the alternative interpretation ("verify: `npm version major` on `1.0.0-rc.N` produces `1.0.0`, not `2.0.0-rc.0`"). **Task 1 verification (mandatory before dispatch):** in a scratch directory, `mkdir -p /tmp/skf-semver-check && cd /tmp/skf-semver-check && npm init -y && jq '.version = "1.0.0-rc.3"' package.json > /tmp/x && mv /tmp/x package.json && npm version major --no-git-tag-version && jq -r .version package.json` SHALL print `1.0.0`. If it prints `2.0.0` or `2.0.0-rc.0`, HALT and use the workflow-override alternative: file a Story 3.x follow-up to add explicit `npm version 1.0.0 --no-git-tag-version` override branch in `release.yaml § Bump version` for the major-from-rc case. **Expected outcome (per node-semver docs + empirical semver spec):** `1.0.0`. Clean up the scratch dir after verification.
22	
23	3. **Pre-publish precondition check — read `package.json` version DIRECTLY from remote `main` (not local clone).** Per Story 5.2 AC 5 stronger-precondition-check pattern: `gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/contents/package.json --jq '.content' | base64 -d | jq -r .version` SHALL return `1.0.0-rc.3`. This short-circuits any local-clone staleness. If it returns anything other than `1.0.0-rc.3`: HALT and investigate — either Story 5.2's reconciliation was not committed, or another cut is in flight. Also confirm: `gh api .../contents/.claude-plugin/marketplace.json --jq '.content' | base64 -d | jq -r '.plugins[0].version'` → `1.0.0-rc.3`. Also confirm: `gh api .../contents/CHANGELOG.md --jq '.content' | base64 -d | grep -c '^## \[1\.0\.0-rc\.3\]'` → `1` (the rc.3 section is present). Also confirm: `gh api .../contents/CHANGELOG.md --jq '.content' | base64 -d | grep -c '^## \[1\.0\.0\] - TBD'` → `1` (the hand-curated placeholder survives for AC 10 reconciliation).
24	
25	4. **`release.yaml` dispatched with `version_bump: major` — only from `main` tip.** After AC 2 + AC 3 pass: (a) `gh workflow run release.yaml -f version_bump=major --ref main` (the `--ref main` is load-bearing per Story 5.2 Dev Notes (D) — non-main dispatches take the legacy tag-only path at `release.yaml:639-647` which is NOT the canonical v1.0.0 cut); (b) capture the run id via `RUN_ID=$(gh run list --workflow=release.yaml --limit 1 --json databaseId --jq '.[0].databaseId')`; (c) monitor via `gh run watch $RUN_ID`; (d) the workflow pauses at TWO gates (Story 3.4 refactor): first the `release` environment gate at job start (Armel approves via UI to release the deployment), second the bot PR review-decision gate before auto-merge (Armel EITHER approves the bot PR via review UI OR admin-bypass-merges — per Story 5.2 pattern, admin-bypass-merge is acceptable and is the observed pattern for PRs #195/#196/#197/#209); (e) the workflow resumes after both gates, creates the tag on the merge commit post-merge, publishes to npm under `--tag latest`, creates the GitHub Release with `prerelease: false`, and emits the step summary; (f) final run conclusion SHALL be `success`.
26	
27	5. **`release.yaml § Bump version` advances `1.0.0-rc.3` → `1.0.0`.** The case-chain at `release.yaml:113-115` routes `version_bump: major` → `npm version major --no-git-tag-version` (the `*) npm version ${{ github.event.inputs.version_bump }} --no-git-tag-version ;;` branch). On `package.json.version == "1.0.0-rc.3"`, this SHALL produce `1.0.0`. **Expected failure mode:** if `Bump version` produces `2.0.0` or errors, AC 2's scratch verification missed a semver edge case — HALT per AC 13 and fix via workflow override (see AC 2). **In-run verification:** monitor the `Get new version and previous tag` step (`release.yaml:118-122`) output — `echo "new_version=..."` SHALL emit `new_version=1.0.0` and `previous_tag=v1.0.0-rc.3`.
28	
29	6. **Provenance attestation published under `--tag latest` (NFR4 100% provenance coverage).** After the workflow completes: `npm view bmad-module-skill-forge@1.0.0 --json | jq '.dist.attestations'` SHALL return a non-null JSON object containing a `provenance.predicateType` of `https://slsa.dev/provenance/v1` (or `/v0.2` — both are SLSA L2; accept either). The attestation URL pattern SHALL match `https://registry.npmjs.org/-/npm/v1/attestations/bmad-module-skill-forge@1.0.0`. A `null` or absent attestation is a CRITICAL BLOCKER — v1.0.0 under `--tag latest` WITHOUT provenance is a direct NFR4 + NFR7 violation. If the attestation is absent, the ONLY acceptable recovery is to IMMEDIATELY `npm deprecate bmad-module-skill-forge@1.0.0 "Critical: provenance absent; use v1.0.1 instead"` and cut v1.0.1 with provenance via a new `release.yaml` dispatch. Per NFR6, `npm unpublish` is NOT an option for v1.0.0 regardless of eligibility window. This is Scenario B of the Rollback Playbook (`docs/RELEASING.md § Scenario B`).
30	
31	7. **Publish dist-tag is `latest`, NOT `rc`.** After the workflow completes: `npm view bmad-module-skill-forge dist-tags --json` SHALL return `{"latest":"1.0.0","alpha":"0.10.1-alpha.0","rc":"1.0.0-rc.3"}` — **`latest` flips from `0.10.0` → `1.0.0`** (expected and INTENDED — this is the whole point of Story 5.3); `alpha` stays at `0.10.1-alpha.0` (unchanged); `rc` stays at `1.0.0-rc.3` (unchanged — Story 5.3's major-cut does NOT bump the rc tag). Also: `npm view bmad-module-skill-forge@latest version` SHALL return `1.0.0`. Per `release.yaml:686-695` case-chain: the new version `1.0.0` contains none of `"alpha"` / `"beta"` / `"rc"` substrings, so falls through to the `else npm publish --tag latest` branch. **Expected failure mode (none anticipated):** if `latest` fails to flip to `1.0.0`, the case-chain substring match is broken — deferred-work.md § 5-2 F1 tracks substring-classification hardening as a post-v1.0.0 item; not Story 5.3 territory unless it actually fires.
32	
33	8. **Workflow side-effects verified on `main` and at `v1.0.0` tag.** After the workflow completes, on `main`: (a) `main` tip advances by TWO commits per Story 3.4 PR-auto-merge flow (matching the rc.3 observation in Story 5.2 audit § Topology note) — the merge commit (bot PR auto-merged) + the `release: bump to v1.0.0` commit underneath; `git log main -1 --format='%H %s'` returns the merge commit; `git log main --grep='^release: bump to v1.0.0$' --oneline` returns the workflow-authored release commit (note `$` anchor to disambiguate from `v1.0.0-rc.*`); (b) `jq -r .version package.json` → `1.0.0` (AC 8b); (c) `jq -r '.plugins[0].version' .claude-plugin/marketplace.json` → `1.0.0` (AC 8c — SECOND live proof of Story 5.1 H3 jq-based marketplace sync working end-to-end after Story 5.2 rc.3 was the first); (d) `grep -cE '^## \[1\.0\.0\]' CHANGELOG.md` → `2` at this point (the auto-generated one from this cut + the stale `## [1.0.0] - TBD` placeholder). AC 10's reconciliation commit deletes the duplicate. (e) `grep -c '^## \[1.0.0-rc.3\]' CHANGELOG.md` → `1` (the rc.3 entry survives, as expected — workflow only prepends, awk preamble-restore doesn't touch older entries); (f) `head -5 CHANGELOG.md` SHALL show `# Changelog` at line 1 + preamble + `## [Unreleased]` (the Story 3.3 awk restore's preamble-preservation works identically to rc.3). And at the tag: (g) `git tag -l 'v1.0.0'` returns `v1.0.0` (note: exact match — NOT `v1.0.0-rc.*`); (h) `gh release view v1.0.0 --json tagName,isPrerelease,body,createdAt,htmlUrl` returns `{"tagName":"v1.0.0","isPrerelease":false,...}` — **`prerelease: false` is load-bearing** because `release.yaml:704` sets prerelease to true only when the version contains `alpha|beta|rc`; `1.0.0` contains none, so it's a stable release (NFR6 threshold).
34	
35	9. **Tag anchors on the merge commit (Story 3.4 PR-auto-merge flow, not the CI-ephemeral release commit).** Per `release.yaml:649-684` (`Create and push tag` step with Story 3.4 P9 anchor-verification), under `github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'` the tag anchors on the PR merge commit (`$TAG_ANCHOR` comes from `gh pr view --json mergeCommit.oid`), NOT on the bot's release commit inside the temp branch. **Verification:** `git rev-parse v1.0.0` SHALL equal the merge commit SHA (the one at `main` tip after the bot PR auto-merges); `git log v1.0.0 -1 --format='%s'` SHALL return the merge commit subject (shape: `Merge pull request #<N> from armelhbobdad/release/bot/v1.0.0-<run_id>`), NOT the `release: bump to v1.0.0` subject. The underlying release commit is reachable via `git log v1.0.0 -2 --oneline` (second line). This topology was established in Story 5.2 for rc.3 (merge SHA `5fad470`; release commit `78ac999`).
36	
37	10. **CHANGELOG v1.0.0 reconciliation in Commit 2 — resolve the TWO `## [1.0.0]` duplicates.** After the workflow completes, `CHANGELOG.md` has TWO `## [1.0.0]` H2 headers: (A) the auto-generated one at the top (position varies depending on awk restore: placed directly below `## [Unreleased]`, shape `## [1.0.0](https://github.com/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/compare/v1.0.0-rc.3...v1.0.0) (2026-04-XX)`, containing conventional-commit-derived `### Features`/`### Bug Fixes` from commits between `v1.0.0-rc.3` and the new `v1.0.0` tag — likely sparse, mostly review-patch chores); (B) the hand-curated stale placeholder at the former line 396 (below `## [1.0.0-rc.3]` block), shape `## [1.0.0] - TBD` with the prose block authored by Story 5.1 (Features/Bug Fixes/Initial Release/Highlights/Workflows table/Tiers/IDE Support/Links). **Reconciliation protocol (Commit 2):** (i) REPLACE the auto-generated `## [1.0.0](...)` block's BODY (the `### Features` / `### Bug Fixes` sub-sections) with the hand-curated prose from block B (Initial Release through Links), preserving the auto-generated HEADER line (so the compare URL + date are verbatim what the workflow emitted — no risk of stale date); (ii) DELETE block B entirely (the `## [1.0.0] - TBD` header + all hand-curated content + any HTML comment preamble) — it has been merged into block A; (iii) post-edit verification: `grep -cE '^## \[1\.0\.0\]' CHANGELOG.md` → `1` (exactly one, the reconciled block); `grep -c '^## \[1\.0\.0\] - TBD' CHANGELOG.md` → `0` (stale placeholder gone); `grep -c '^## \[1\.0\.0\](https' CHANGELOG.md` → `1` (the reconciled block has the compare URL per Story 5.1's deferred-work line 117: "Story 5.3 final-cut reconciliation should add the `compare/v0.10.0...v1.0.0` URL when replacing `TBD` with the publish date"). Note: the compare URL IS in the auto-generated header; the reconciliation preserves it. (iv) Keep the `## [1.0.0-rc.3]` block untouched — it is historical and immutable once published.
38	
39	11. **Audit artifact sign-off append.** Commit 2 modifies `release-audits/v1.0.0-launch-audit.md`: append a new H2 section `## Story 5.3 v1.0.0 Final Cut` AFTER `## Story 5.2 RC Cut + Smoke Test` (becomes the new bottom-most H2) containing these sub-sections: (i) `### Workflow dispatch run` — the `gh run view` URL + trigger / env-approval / pr-approval-or-admin-bypass-merge / completion timestamps + final `conclusion: success`; (ii) `### Bot PR auto-merge evidence` — the bot PR URL + merge-commit SHA on main + auto-merge vs admin-bypass-merge attestation (match the Story 5.2 rc.3 pattern — admin-bypass-merge is the expected path); (iii) `### npm publish evidence` — verbatim excerpted fields from `npm view bmad-module-skill-forge@1.0.0 --json | jq '{version, dist: {tarball, shasum, attestations}}'`; pre-cut vs post-cut `npm view bmad-module-skill-forge dist-tags --json` snapshots showing `latest: 0.10.0 → 1.0.0`, `rc: 1.0.0-rc.3` unchanged, `alpha: 0.10.1-alpha.0` unchanged; the updated `npm view versions --json` list (prior `["...","1.0.0-rc.3"]` now appended with `"1.0.0"`); (iv) `### GitHub Release v1.0.0` — verbatim output of `gh release view v1.0.0 --json tagName,isPrerelease,name,createdAt,htmlUrl,body` (the full body since v1.0.0 is the headline release — the hand-curated release notes are the record); (v) `### Main-branch side-effects` — the AC 8 (a)–(h) outputs verbatim + AC 9's tag-anchor verification; (vi) `### CHANGELOG reconciliation` — the `grep -c` counts from AC 10 (iii) + a before/after diff excerpt showing the merge of blocks A and B; (vii) `### NFR1 cycle-time record` — wall-clock delta for this dispatch (the 2nd data point in the rolling-10-release series per Story 5.2 audit § NFR1); (viii) `### NFR6 v1.0.0 immutability activation** — verbatim sentence: `v1.0.0 is live on npm under --tag latest as of <ISO-8601 UTC timestamp>. NFR6 immutability policy is now in force: this version SHALL NEVER be unpublished or republished; rollback uses npm deprecate + ship forward per docs/RELEASING.md § Scenario A/B.`; (ix) `### Decision: v1.0.0 launch status` — verbatim `LAUNCHED — v1.0.0 is live on npm under --tag latest with SLSA L2 provenance. Story 5.4 cross-platform verification cleared to begin (NFR9: within 1h of publish).` AND update the existing top-level `## Sign-off` block (line 199-201) to append a second sentence: `v1.0.0 launched 2026-MM-DD HH:MMZ; Story 5.4 cross-platform verification in scope.` (keep the Story 5.2 rc.3 sentence intact — this is an additive append).
40	
41	12. **Quality gate (`npm run quality`) passes on Commit 2.** Before opening the PR for Commit 2: `npm run quality` SHALL exit 0 — all 13 subcommands green. Main failure modes for this specific commit: (a) `lint:md` — `CHANGELOG.md` + `release-audits/v1.0.0-launch-audit.md` + `docs/RELEASING.md` all run through markdownlint-cli2; any non-conforming heading shapes (ATX-style, one-space-after-hash) or broken table pipes in the reconciled v1.0.0 block will fail; (b) `validate:refs` — new links added to the audit artifact's sign-off section SHALL resolve to real commits/releases/runs on GitHub (the workflow run URL, the bot PR URL, the tag URL, the GitHub Release URL must all be reachable); (c) `lint:md` on `docs/RELEASING.md` — the `### Cutting v1.0.0 under --tag latest` section replaces the prior `### Cutting v1.0.0-rc.1` section; ensure the new section follows the same five-element shape (Trigger / CLI / Outcome / Constraints / Verification) as Scenarios A–G for consistency; (d) `prettier` — `CHANGELOG.md` is prettier-controlled; any hand-edits that drift from prettier's canonical form for markdown will fail. If any sub-check fails: iterate the edits until green BEFORE opening the PR (do NOT open a PR with local quality-gate failures — the required CI checks will block merge and burn a cycle).
42	
43	13. **Blocker protocol — if Story 5.3 cannot proceed cleanly.** Mirror of Story 5.2 AC 21 logic with v1.0.0-specific intensification: if the workflow dispatch fails mid-run (any step exits non-zero), HALT and report — DO NOT retry-until-green on a failing workflow; investigate the failing step first. Given Story 5.2's 7-attempt saga already drove the workflow through 5 latent defects and fixed them, a Story 5.3 failure SHOULD be much rarer — but if one occurs, the defect-discovery protocol is: (a) file a same-repo GitHub issue with title `v1.0.0 release blocker: <one-line>`; (b) fix on a new feature branch → PR → merge to `main`; (c) **the re-dispatch produces a NEW version number** — this is where Story 5.3 differs from Story 5.2's rc.N iteration loop. A v1.0.0 cut that fails mid-pipeline AFTER the `Bump version` step runs has committed `1.0.0` to the temp branch; whether to re-dispatch as `1.0.0` or advance to `1.0.1` depends on the failure step: (i) failure BEFORE the bot PR auto-merges → temp branch is orphaned; delete it + re-dispatch with `version_bump: major` on `1.0.0-rc.3` → produces `1.0.0` again (same version, clean retry, recoverable per Scenario D of the Rollback Playbook); (ii) failure AFTER the bot PR auto-merges to main BUT BEFORE npm publish succeeds → `main` now has the `release: bump to v1.0.0` commit + `v1.0.0` tag pushed + NO npm artifact; this is Scenario D territory (`git tag -d v1.0.0 && git push --delete origin v1.0.0`); re-dispatch with `version_bump: patch` on `1.0.0` → produces `1.0.1` (the version number `1.0.0` is NOT burned on npm — `npm view bmad-module-skill-forge@1.0.0` returns 404 — so `1.0.0` could be reclaimed, BUT per NFR6 do not reclaim — ship `1.0.1` forward); (iii) failure AFTER npm publish succeeds but BEFORE GitHub Release creation → `v1.0.0` is live on npm with provenance; this is Scenario E (`gh release create v1.0.0 ...` manual recovery) — the published artifact stands, manual reconciliation completes the release. Per NFR6, once `npm publish --tag latest` succeeds for `1.0.0`, the version is IMMUTABLE — no retry, no re-cut, no unpublish. If failure is outside Story 5.3's fix scope (systemic npm outage, GitHub OIDC regression), mark as external-blocker, pause, and resume when upstream is healthy.
44	
45	14. **`docs/RELEASING.md § Cutting v1.0.0-rc.1` → `§ Cutting v1.0.0 under --tag latest` title + content flip in Commit 2** (per `deferred-work.md § 5-2 Round 2` line 132). The existing section (`docs/RELEASING.md:559-616`) documents the hand-bump + rc-cut pattern that Stories 5.1 and 5.2 used; it is single-use historical documentation of a one-time event. Story 5.3 replaces it with a `### Cutting v1.0.0 under --tag latest` section documenting the final promotion path, same five-element shape: (a) **Trigger.** RC survived clean smoke test (`release-audits/v1.0.0-launch-audit.md § Story 5.2 RC Cut + Smoke Test § Decision`: `PASS`); `npm view bmad-module-skill-forge@rc version` returns the passing RC; Story 5.3 ready to promote to `latest` under manual approval gate. (b) **CLI.** `gh workflow run release.yaml -f version_bump=major --ref main` (dispatches from `main` tip; `--ref main` load-bearing per Story 5.2 D); monitor via `gh run watch`; approve at the `release` env gate; approve OR admin-bypass-merge the bot PR; workflow auto-tags, publishes under `--tag latest`, creates GitHub Release. Expected ~5-8 min wall-clock if gates approved quickly. (c) **Expected outcome.** `main` tip advances by 2 commits (merge + release); `jq -r .version package.json` → `1.0.0`; `jq -r '.plugins[0].version' .claude-plugin/marketplace.json` → `1.0.0`; `npm view bmad-module-skill-forge dist-tags.latest` → `1.0.0`; `rc` dist-tag UNCHANGED at the prior RC; SLSA L2 provenance attached; GitHub Release `v1.0.0` with `prerelease: false`. (d) **Constraints.** Execute ONLY after RC audit Sign-off is populated AND the smoke-test Decision is `PASS`. The cut is IRREVERSIBLE — once `npm publish --tag latest` succeeds, NFR6 forbids unpublish regardless of eligibility window; rollback is `npm deprecate` + ship forward (Scenario A/B). The CHANGELOG reconciliation is a POST-workflow step (Commit 2); do NOT hand-edit the `## [1.0.0] - TBD` placeholder BEFORE dispatch — let the workflow auto-generate its block, then reconcile in one commit. (e) **Verification.** Include the AC 6 / AC 7 / AC 8 / AC 10 verification greps + the NFR6 immutability activation timestamp. Keep the existing five-element-shape scaffolding that Story 5.1 authored. Remove Story 5.1's pre-Story-3.4 prose about the non-PR-auto-merge flow (the "Story 3.4 refactor note — PR-auto-merge flow" appendix at `docs/RELEASING.md:614-616` can be absorbed into the main section body since PR-auto-merge is now the canonical flow).
46	
47	15. **Commit hygiene on Commit 2.** Subject: `docs(release): reconcile CHANGELOG v1.0.0 and sign off publication (Story 5.3)` (64 chars — under the 72-char limit). Body requirements: (a) reference the workflow run URL absolutely; (b) reference the bot PR URL + merge commit SHA; (c) reference the GitHub Release `v1.0.0` URL; (d) list the three files touched (`CHANGELOG.md`, `release-audits/v1.0.0-launch-audit.md`, `docs/RELEASING.md`) with per-file purpose one-liners; (e) NO `Fixes #NN` trailer (per user-memory `feedback_no_issue_numbers_in_pr.md`); (f) NO `_bmad-output/todo/` references; (g) `AC N` reference form (NOT `#N`) if ACs are cited; (h) one short paragraph describing the CHANGELOG reconciliation decision (merging blocks A + B into a single reconciled entry); (i) commit-msg + pre-commit hooks pass without `--no-verify`.
48	
49	16. **Branch-protection compliance — Commit 2 goes through PR, not direct push.** Per Story 5.1 audit Row 9 + Story 5.2 AC 17: direct push to `main` is blocked. Commit 2 is authored on a fresh branch `feat/v1-final-signoff` (fresh from `origin/main` tip AFTER the workflow-authored release commits land — wait for the bot PR auto-merge to complete BEFORE creating this branch), pushed via PR, gated by all 7 required status checks + review approval (self-approve or admin-bypass-merge, matching the rc.3 pattern for PRs #210 + #212).
50	
51	17. **Sprint-status update at completion.** After Commit 2 is merged to `main`: update `_bmad-output/implementation-artifacts/sprint-status.yaml` — flip `5-3-cut-v1-0-0-under-tag-latest-with-manual-approval` from `in-progress` → `review` (by dev-story at Commit 2 branch creation) → `done` (by code-review once approved). This follows the standard Dev → Review → Done flow; Story 5.3's dev-story phase updates the status per standard sprint-tracking conventions. Note: Epic 5 status remains `in-progress` at Story 5.3 completion — Story 5.4 is still backlog; Epic 5 flips to `done` only after 5.4 closes (per sprint-status header rules).
52	
53	18. **NFR6 immutability annotation — audit artifact captures the policy activation.** Per AC 11 (viii): the audit artifact's `## Story 5.3 v1.0.0 Final Cut § NFR6 v1.0.0 immutability activation` subsection records the verbatim timestamp at which `npm publish --tag latest` succeeded. From that instant: any future defect in `1.0.0` is resolved via `npm deprecate bmad-module-skill-forge@1.0.0 "..."` + shipping `1.0.1` forward — NEVER `npm unpublish`. This annotation is the forensic record that the policy activation was deliberate, witnessed by an approver, and timestamped for audit replay.
54	
55	19. **Runtime / npm floor reconfirmation (NFR8 reconfirm).** The `release.yaml § Verify npm version floor for OIDC trusted publishing` step at lines 80-93 is the runtime enforcement (npm ≥11.5.1). `.nvmrc` pins Node to `22` (LTS channel; GitHub Actions `setup-node` resolves to latest 22.x, which is ≥22.14.0). The workflow self-asserts; no Story 5.3 action required BEYOND verifying that the dispatch succeeds through that step. Pre-dispatch check: `cat .nvmrc` SHALL return `22` (or a value ≥22.14.0).
56	
57	20. **Zero new files created in Story 5.3.** Unlike Story 5.1 (created `docs/v1.0.0-launch-audit.md`, later moved to `release-audits/v1.0.0-launch-audit.md`), Story 5.3 creates ZERO new files. It MODIFIES three files in Commit 2 (`CHANGELOG.md`, `release-audits/v1.0.0-launch-audit.md`, `docs/RELEASING.md`). Workflow-side-effect modifications on `package.json`, `.claude-plugin/marketplace.json`, `CHANGELOG.md` (auto-generated block) are NOT Story 5.3 file-scope. **Verification:** `git log main --grep='Story 5.3' --name-only --format='%H %s'` enumerates exactly Commit 2's file list; no `A <path>` (added) entries.
58	
59	## Tasks / Subtasks

62	  - [x] `git fetch origin && git checkout main && git pull` — local `main` at latest `origin/main` tip

64	  - [x] `gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/contents/package.json --jq '.content' | base64 -d | jq -r .version` → `1.0.0-rc.3` (AC 3 stronger precondition check from REMOTE, not local)

If anything other than `1.0.0`: HALT — workflow needs an explicit `npm version 1.0.0 --no-git-tag-version` override branch for `major`-from-rc case (new Story 3.x); file issue and stop. Clean up `/tmp/skf-semver-check` after verification.
74	  - [x] Document all recon results verbatim in Dev Agent Record § Debug Log References

Match the rc.3 pattern (PRs #209 bypass-merged). Record the bot PR number + approval/merge timestamp.

87	  - [x] Wait for run completion: `gh run view $RUN_ID --json status,conclusion --jq '{status,conclusion}'` → `{"status":"completed","conclusion":"success"}`. Record completion timestamp (NFR1 end marker).
88	  - [x] Compute wall-clock delta: (completion - start) vs NFR1 target (20 min total). This is the 2nd data point in the rolling-10-release NFR1 series (Story 5.2 rc.3 was the 1st at 5m 4s).
89	  - [x] If conclusion != success: HALT per AC 13. Capture the failing step + logs. Route per AC 13 (i)/(ii)/(iii) depending on which step failed.
